capability.questionContext: METR, an AI safety research organization, published research in March 2025 showing that the length of tasks AI agents can reliably complete has been doubling approximately every 7 months for the past 6 years. At that time, frontier models could reliably handle autonomous tasks taking humans about an hour. If the trend holds, AI agents would handle week-long tasks by 2027 and month-long projects by the end of the decade. Each new capability threshold means more tasks that are cheaper and faster to delegate to AI than to do yourself — and once a task is delegated, the human skill to do it starts to atrophy.

source.label: METR — Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks (March 2025)

singularity.answerLabel: Yes — it keeps doubling roughly every 7 months
singularity.answerDesc: Scaling laws hold, compute keeps growing, and AI capability maintains its exponential trajectory.

timelineEvent.headline: AI scaling trend holds
timelineEvent.description: The doubling trend that began in 2020 shows no signs of bending. Each generation of models is meaningfully more capable than the last. Frontier labs race to build the next generation, and the exponential trajectory continues.

stalls.answerLabel: No — it slows down or hits a wall
stalls.answerDesc: Something breaks the trend — diminishing returns on scaling, data limitations, architectural ceilings, or something unexpected — and AI capability plateaus before reaching superhuman levels.

timelineEvent.headline: AI scaling hits a wall
timelineEvent.description: The exponential trend breaks. Whether from data limits, compute costs, or fundamental barriers — AI capability plateaus. The singularity recedes from view.

stall_duration.questionText: Where does autonomous AI capability top out?
stall_duration.questionContext: If the exponential trend doesn't hold, AI will hit a ceiling somewhere. The question is where. Think not about what AI can do with constant human guidance, but what it can handle reliably on its own — start to finish, no hand-holding.

hours.answerLabel: Around where we are now — hours at best
hours.answerDesc: AI stays great at short tasks but never reliably handles longer autonomous work. The copilot era is the final era.

timelineEvent.headline: AI tops out at hour-level tasks
timelineEvent.description: AI can handle tasks requiring up to a few hours of focused work, but nothing beyond. Useful, but not transformative. The world adjusts to a tool, not a revolution.

days.answerDesc: AI can reliably handle a full day's worth of autonomous work, but falls apart on anything requiring multi-day coherence.

timelineEvent.headline: AI tops out at day-level tasks
timelineEvent.description: AI handles tasks requiring a full day of focused effort — research summaries, code modules, analysis. Significant automation, but human expertise remains essential for complex work.

weeks.answerDesc: AI handles week-long autonomous projects capably, but month-long endeavors requiring sustained strategic judgment remain out of reach.

timelineEvent.headline: AI tops out at week-level tasks
timelineEvent.description: AI can manage week-long projects autonomously — full research cycles, software features, strategic analyses. A powerful tool that reshapes knowledge work, but plateaus short of full generality.

months.answerDesc: AI reaches near-AGI capability for most knowledge work but hits a hard ceiling before full open-ended autonomy or recursive self-improvement.

timelineEvent.headline: AI tops out at month-level tasks
timelineEvent.description: AI handles month-long autonomous projects — entire product developments, research programs, organizational analyses. Remarkably capable, but something prevents the final leap to open-ended superintelligence.

stall_recovery.questionText: What is required for progress to resume?
stall_recovery.questionContext: AI capability has hit a ceiling — but ceilings in technology have been broken before. The history of computing is full of walls that turned out to be architectural limitations, not fundamental ones. New paradigms (transistors replacing vacuum tubes, GPUs replacing CPUs for training, transformers replacing RNNs) have repeatedly restarted progress after apparent dead ends. Researchers like Yann LeCun have argued that current architectures are fundamentally missing key ingredients — world models that let systems simulate consequences, plan ahead, and reason about cause and effect — and that genuinely new approaches (like his proposed JEPA architecture) could eventually break through where scaling alone cannot. The question is whether the current ceiling is architectural or fundamental — and if architectural, how long until someone finds the door.

mild.answerLabel: A mild breakthrough — months to a few years
mild.answerDesc: The ceiling is real but thin. A new training technique, architectural tweak, or compute breakthrough restarts the exponential trend within a few years. A speed bump, not a wall.

timelineEvent.headline: A mild breakthrough restarts progress
timelineEvent.description: A new technique, dataset, or architectural insight unlocks the next phase of capability growth. The stall was temporary — months to a few years — and the trajectory resumes.

substantial.answerLabel: A substantial breakthrough — years to decades
substantial.answerDesc: The ceiling is fundamental to current approaches. Breaking through requires a genuine paradigm shift — new architectures, new theory, maybe new hardware. It could take a decade or more. The world builds around current AI capability in the meantime.

timelineEvent.headline: A major breakthrough eventually arrives
timelineEvent.description: Progress requires a fundamental rethinking — new paradigms, new hardware, or insights from adjacent fields. The gap lasts years to decades before capability growth resumes.

never.answerDesc: The ceiling isn't architectural — it's fundamental. There's something about intelligence, reasoning, or generality that current approaches can't reach and no foreseeable approach will. This is as good as AI gets.

timelineEvent.headline: The ceiling is permanent
timelineEvent.description: No breakthrough comes. AI capability has reached its fundamental limit. The technology is powerful but bounded — and the world adapts to that reality.

plateau_benefit_distribution.questionText: Who benefits from the AI plateau?
plateau_benefit_distribution.questionContext: AI has hit its ceiling — but even at this level, it's the most powerful tool ever built. Entire industries have already reorganized around it. The economic question isn't whether AI transforms the economy — it already has. The question is who captures the value. Does the productivity surge flow broadly, or do a narrowing set of people capture most of the gains? The answer depends on policy, market structure, and whether the institutions that redistribute gains can keep pace with the technology that's concentrating them.

equal.answerDesc: Productivity gains flow through as lower costs, better services, and public investment. New policies and market dynamics ensure the benefits of AI reach most people. Not utopia, but genuine broad-based improvement.

timelineEvent.headline: AI gains shared broadly
timelineEvent.description: Productivity gains from the AI plateau flow broadly across the economy. New policies, market forces, or social norms ensure that automation's benefits don't concentrate.

unequal.answerLabel: Unequally — existing wealth holders benefit most
unequal.answerDesc: Those who already own capital — the companies that trained the models, the firms that deployed them — capture most of the value. Labor's share of income falls as routine work is automated. A new gilded age, built on models and compute.

timelineEvent.headline: AI gains flow to existing wealth holders
timelineEvent.description: Those who already hold capital and infrastructure capture most of the value from AI automation. The plateau entrenches existing economic hierarchies.

extreme.answerLabel: Extremely unequally — those holding power capture nearly everything
extreme.answerDesc: The gains concentrate not just among the wealthy, but among those who hold political and institutional power at the time of transformation. Regulatory capture, monopoly control of compute, and state-corporate fusion ensure that a tiny elite controls the AI-driven economy. Everyone else is a dependent.

timelineEvent.headline: AI-driven power concentration
timelineEvent.description: Those controlling AI infrastructure capture nearly all the economic value. The plateau doesn't just maintain inequality — it amplifies it to historic extremes.

plateau_knowledge_rate.questionText: How much knowledge work does AI at this level actually replace?
plateau_knowledge_rate.questionContext: AI has hit its ceiling — but even at this level, it's enormously capable within its range. The question isn't whether AI changes knowledge work; it already has. The question is how thoroughly. Does it replace entire roles, or augment them? Does adoption happen in a rush, or does institutional inertia slow things down? Professional licensing, union contracts, regulation, and cultural resistance all shape the pace — but so does the sheer economic pressure of a tool that's cheaper and faster than humans at everything within its reach.

rapid.answerLabel: Rapid — within AI's range, most work automated in 2–5 years
rapid.answerDesc: The economic pressure is overwhelming. Any knowledge task within AI's capability ceiling is automated within 2 to 5 years. Entire roles disappear. The ceiling limits what AI can do, but within those limits, adoption is swift and total.

timelineEvent.headline: Knowledge work rapidly automated
timelineEvent.description: Within AI's capability range, most routine knowledge work is automated within 2–5 years. The labor market transforms quickly for affected sectors.

gradual.answerDesc: Institutional inertia slows things down. AI is deployed sector by sector, firm by firm over 5 to 15 years. Some industries adopt fast, others resist. The ceiling is fixed, but even reaching it takes a decade.

timelineEvent.headline: Knowledge automation unfolds gradually
timelineEvent.description: Adoption unfolds over 5–15 years as organizations, regulations, and workers adapt. The transformation is real but measured.

uneven.answerLabel: Uneven — 2–5 years in tech, 10–20+ in lagging sectors
uneven.answerDesc: Tech-forward industries and wealthy nations automate within a few years. Regulated professions, developing economies, and culturally resistant sectors lag by a decade or more. A patchwork of adoption against a fixed capability ceiling.

timelineEvent.headline: Knowledge automation is uneven
timelineEvent.description: Tech and finance automate within 2–5 years. Education, healthcare, government lag by a decade or more. Two economies emerge within one society.

limited.answerLabel: Limited — AI augments but rarely replaces
limited.answerDesc: The capability ceiling is real and binding. AI assists knowledge workers but rarely replaces them outright. Most roles absorb AI as a tool rather than being eliminated by it. Productivity rises, but employment patterns shift more than they collapse. No displacement wave — just a permanent productivity boost.

timelineEvent.headline: AI augments but rarely replaces
timelineEvent.description: Despite impressive demos, AI mostly augments human workers rather than replacing them. Productivity grows but the labor market transforms less than predicted.

plateau_physical_rate.questionText: Does physical automation advance alongside AI, or does it lag?
plateau_physical_rate.questionContext: AI capability has plateaued for knowledge work — but the physical world is a separate frontier. Robotics, autonomous vehicles, and automated manufacturing depend on AI but also on hardware, engineering, and infrastructure that follow their own trajectory. Physical automation could advance independently of whether AI gets smarter. Or the AI plateau could slow robotics investment and innovation too. The question is whether the physical world transforms alongside the digital one, or stays largely human.

gradual.answerLabel: Gradual — physical automation trails by 10–25 years
gradual.answerDesc: Physical automation advances, but slowly — over 10 to 25 years. The AI plateau means fewer breakthroughs in robotic capability. Manufacturing, logistics, and agriculture are partially automated; the rest of the physical world remains human for decades.

timelineEvent.headline: Physical automation trails by decades
timelineEvent.description: Robots, autonomous vehicles, and manufacturing automation follow 10–25 years behind knowledge work automation. The physical world changes, slowly.

uneven.answerLabel: Uneven — 5–10 years in manufacturing hubs, 20+ elsewhere
uneven.answerDesc: Physical automation advances in pockets — wealthy nations with strong manufacturing and high labor costs see results within a decade. Developing economies and rural areas see little change for 20+ years. The AI plateau amplifies existing global inequalities in physical infrastructure.

timelineEvent.headline: Physical automation is geographically uneven
timelineEvent.description: Manufacturing hubs automate within 5–10 years. Rural areas and developing regions wait 20+ years. Geography becomes destiny.

limited.answerLabel: Limited — physical work remains largely human indefinitely
limited.answerDesc: The AI plateau means robotics stagnates too. Without more capable AI, physical automation stays limited to structured environments — factories, warehouses. The unstructured physical world remains human territory for the foreseeable future.

timelineEvent.headline: Physical work stays largely human
timelineEvent.description: Despite AI advances in knowledge work, the physical world resists automation. Dexterity, unpredictability, and infrastructure costs keep human labor dominant.

takeoff.questionText: How much does each generation of AI accelerate the next?
takeoff.questionContext: AI is now automating its own research — each new generation helps build the next one. The key question is how strong this feedback loop is. At the baseline METR doubling rate of ~7 months, the journey from current AI (~12-hour tasks) to superintelligence (~year-long tasks) would take over 5 years with no acceleration. But if each doubling of AI capability makes the next doubling come faster, the timeline compresses dramatically — to about 2 years at 20% acceleration per cycle, or 7 months at 50%.

source.label: AI 2027 — AI R&D Progress Multiplier (AI Futures Project, April 2025)

none.answerDesc: AI automates research but doesn't speed up its own improvement cycle. Each doubling still takes ~7 months. The path to superintelligence stretches over 5 years. Institutions can keep up. Governance frameworks have time to mature. The world adapts gradually.

timelineEvent.headline: AI research progresses at baseline rate
timelineEvent.description: AI automates much of the research process, but each generation doesn't meaningfully accelerate the next. Progress continues at the baseline ~7-month doubling rate — powerful, but predictable. The path to superintelligence stretches over half a decade.

slow.answerDesc: Each generation of AI makes the next one come slightly faster. The doubling time gradually shrinks from 7 months toward 3. Total time to superintelligence: about 3–4 years. Fast by historical standards, but slow enough for governance to keep pace.

timelineEvent.headline: AI research gradually accelerates
timelineEvent.description: Each generation of AI modestly accelerates the next. The doubling time compresses from 7 months toward 3 over several years. Institutions strain but can adapt. The acceleration is visible but not overwhelming.

moderate.answerDesc: The feedback loop is real and visible. Each generation makes the next come noticeably faster. The doubling time compresses from 7 months to under 2. Total time to superintelligence: about 2 years. Fast enough to be alarming, slow enough that people see it coming.

timelineEvent.headline: The acceleration becomes undeniable
timelineEvent.description: Each AI generation meaningfully speeds up the next. What started as 7-month cycles compresses to months, then weeks. The pace is clearly accelerating — but there's still time to react.

fast.answerDesc: The R&D feedback loop is powerful. Each generation comes dramatically faster than the last. The doubling time plummets from 7 months to weeks within a year. Superintelligence arrives in roughly a year — the AI 2027 scenario. Most institutions can't adapt. Governance is reactive at best.

timelineEvent.headline: The intelligence explosion
timelineEvent.description: The feedback loop between AI capability and AI research accelerates sharply. Each generation produces the next in a fraction of the time. Within roughly a year, AI leaps from automated research to superintelligence.

explosive.answerDesc: True recursive self-improvement. Each doubling halves the time to the next one — 7 months, then 3.5, then under 2, then weeks, then days. Total time to superintelligence: about 7 months, with the final leap happening in days. Nobody outside the leading lab fully grasps what happened until it's over.

timelineEvent.headline: Runaway intelligence explosion
timelineEvent.description: Each generation of AI produces the next in half the time. The first doublings take months; the last take days. The transition from 'impressive AI' to 'vastly superhuman' compresses into weeks at the end.

agi_threshold.questionText: At what task length does AI match the best humans?
agi_threshold.questionContext: METR measures AI capability by the length of tasks models can reliably complete autonomously. But raw task length doesn't automatically translate to human-competitive performance. A system that handles 24-hour tasks might still lack the judgment, creativity, or contextual understanding that makes top human professionals effective. Or it might already be competitive — the threshold could be lower than we think. The question is where on the capability ladder AI becomes genuinely competitive with the best humans across knowledge work.

twenty_four_hours.answerDesc: Reliably executing day-long autonomous tasks is enough. At that level, AI matches or exceeds most human professionals in structured knowledge work — research, analysis, coding, writing. The bar for human-competitive turns out to be lower than expected.

timelineEvent.headline: AI matches top humans at day-long tasks
timelineEvent.description: AI systems that can reliably handle 24-hour autonomous tasks prove competitive with the best human professionals across most knowledge work domains.

one_week.answerDesc: Day-long tasks aren't enough — human expertise involves sustained coherence across a week of work: revising, iterating, adapting to feedback. AI becomes truly competitive when it can manage week-long projects end to end without losing the thread.

timelineEvent.headline: AI matches top humans at week-long tasks
timelineEvent.description: AI systems reach human-competitive performance once they can sustain coherent, autonomous work across an entire week — managing complexity, revising, and adapting.

few_months.answerLabel: ~A few months — deep expertise takes time
few_months.answerDesc: Real human expertise involves months of sustained strategic thinking — building mental models, navigating ambiguity, adapting to changing circumstances. AI doesn't match this until it can manage multi-month autonomous projects with genuine strategic coherence.

timelineEvent.headline: AI matches top humans at month-long projects
timelineEvent.description: AI reaches human-competitive capability only when it can sustain months of autonomous strategic work — navigating ambiguity and adapting to changing circumstances.

one_year.answerDesc: The best human professionals operate on year-long timescales — building relationships, developing institutional knowledge, executing multi-phase strategies. AI isn't truly competitive until it can match this kind of sustained, adaptive, long-horizon performance.

timelineEvent.headline: AI matches top humans at year-long work
timelineEvent.description: Only when AI can sustain year-long autonomous projects — with strategic adaptation, relationship-building, and institutional understanding — does it truly match the best humans.

ten_plus_years.answerDesc: True human expertise is built over decades — intuition honed through thousands of edge cases, judgment shaped by years of failure and success. AI would need to match decade-scale accumulated wisdom to be genuinely competitive with the best humans.

timelineEvent.headline: AI matches top humans only at decade-scale mastery
timelineEvent.description: The threshold for human-competitive AI turns out to be extraordinarily high — matching the accumulated wisdom and judgment that top humans build over decades of practice.

never.answerLabel: Never — the gap is qualitative, not quantitative
never.answerDesc: No task length closes the gap. What makes the best humans competitive isn't how long they can work autonomously — it's something AI fundamentally lacks: embodied understanding, social intelligence, genuine creativity, or some quality we can't yet name. Task length is the wrong metric entirely.

timelineEvent.headline: AI never truly matches the best humans
timelineEvent.description: Despite handling ever-longer tasks, AI never becomes genuinely competitive with top human professionals. The gap turns out to be qualitative — something that scaling task length alone cannot bridge.

asi_threshold.questionText: At what task length does AI surpass the best humans at knowledge work?
asi_threshold.questionContext: Matching the best humans is one threshold. Surpassing them is another — and it's the one that reshapes everything. When AI doesn't just compete with top professionals but consistently outperforms them, the dynamics of research, strategy, governance, and economic competition fundamentally change. The question is whether raw task-length capability translates into genuine superhuman performance, and if so, at what level.

twenty_four_hours.answerDesc: Even at day-long task capability, AI already surpasses humans in ways that matter — perfect recall, tireless execution, and the ability to run thousands of parallel threads. The superhuman advantage is less about depth and more about breadth and speed.

timelineEvent.headline: AI surpasses top humans at day-long tasks
timelineEvent.description: AI systems handling 24-hour tasks already outperform the best humans — not through deeper understanding, but through parallelism, speed, and consistency that no human can match.

one_week.answerDesc: At week-long capability, AI doesn't just match humans — it surpasses them through relentless consistency, the ability to hold vastly more context, and zero fatigue. Human judgment still has edges, but AI's raw throughput and coherence across a full week overwhelms them.

timelineEvent.headline: AI surpasses top humans at week-long tasks
timelineEvent.description: AI systems managing week-long projects consistently outperform the best human professionals — through sustained focus, perfect memory, and the ability to explore solution spaces no human could cover.

few_months.answerDesc: Superhuman performance requires multi-month strategic coherence. At that level, AI can hold entire organizational strategies in working memory, run multi-phase plans, and adapt faster than any human team. The advantage is no longer just speed — it's qualitatively better judgment across complex, long-horizon problems.

timelineEvent.headline: AI surpasses top humans at multi-month projects
timelineEvent.description: At multi-month capability, AI demonstrates qualitatively superior strategic thinking — managing complexity, adapting to changing conditions, and producing insights no human team could match.

one_year.answerDesc: Surpassing the best humans requires year-long capability — the ability to execute multi-phase strategies with sustained coherence, adapt to cascading changes, and integrate knowledge across domains at a level that makes human expertise look narrow by comparison.

timelineEvent.headline: AI surpasses top humans at year-long work
timelineEvent.description: Only at year-long task capability does AI truly surpass the best humans — demonstrating strategic depth, cross-domain synthesis, and adaptive planning that makes human expertise look narrow.

ten_plus_years.answerLabel: ~10+ years — matching decades of accumulated wisdom
ten_plus_years.answerDesc: Surpassing humans requires matching and exceeding the kind of deep wisdom built over decades — intuition, judgment under radical uncertainty, and the ability to see patterns that only emerge over very long timescales. The bar for superhuman is extraordinarily high.

timelineEvent.headline: AI surpasses top humans only at decade-scale depth
timelineEvent.description: The bar for superhuman performance turns out to be extraordinarily high — requiring AI to match and exceed the accumulated wisdom and judgment that humans build over lifetimes.

never.answerLabel: Never — AI never truly surpasses the best humans
never.answerDesc: AI becomes a powerful tool — faster, more consistent, tireless. But it never surpasses the best humans in ways that matter. The qualities that make top human professionals exceptional — creativity, wisdom, social intelligence, moral judgment — remain beyond AI regardless of task length. Superhuman AI turns out to be a category error.

timelineEvent.headline: AI never surpasses the best humans
timelineEvent.description: Despite ever-growing task capability, AI never truly exceeds the best human professionals at the work that matters most. The superhuman threshold is never crossed.

automation_recovery.questionText: Could a different breakthrough crack the barrier?
automation_recovery.questionContext: The scaling trend didn't produce human-competitive AI — but that doesn't necessarily mean the barrier is permanent. The ceiling might be specific to current architectures, not fundamental. Systems today excel at pattern matching and interpolation but lack the world models, causal reasoning, and planning capabilities that original research demands. A genuinely different approach — learned world models, neurosymbolic architectures, or paradigms not yet invented — could potentially break through where scaling alone couldn't. This is a separate question from whether the trend continues: it's about whether an entirely new paradigm eventually closes the gap.

mild.answerLabel: Yes — within months to a few years
mild.answerDesc: The barrier between routine and creative work turns out to be thin. A new approach cracks it relatively quickly, and AI begins automating novel research and its own development. The feedback loop begins after a delay.

timelineEvent.headline: Deep automation arrives within months
timelineEvent.description: A breakthrough closes the gap. Within months to a few years, AI systems can automate novel creative and research work. The shallow ceiling was temporary.

substantial.answerLabel: Eventually — but it takes years to decades
substantial.answerDesc: The barrier is real and thick. Progress continues slowly, and the breakthrough comes — but only after a long stretch where routine automation is the ceiling. The world adapts to that ceiling before it breaks.

timelineEvent.headline: Deep automation eventually arrives
timelineEvent.description: It takes years to decades, but AI eventually crosses the threshold into genuine creative and research automation. The delay shaped a generation of human-AI collaboration.

never.answerDesc: Novel creative work, original research, and recursive self-improvement remain permanently beyond AI. The routine automation plateau is the final state. Transformative, but not transcendent.

timelineEvent.headline: Deep automation never materializes
timelineEvent.description: The ceiling is permanent. AI remains a powerful assistant for routine tasks but never achieves the creative, research, and reasoning capabilities needed for deep automation.

auto_benefit_distribution.questionText: Who captures the economic value from AI automation?
auto_benefit_distribution.questionContext: AI automates routine knowledge work across every industry. Productivity soars. But productivity gains don't distribute themselves — someone captures them. Historically, the gains from automation have flowed disproportionately to capital owners, with workers eventually benefiting through lower prices and new industries, but often after prolonged pain. AI automation could follow the same pattern, or policy and market structure could steer toward broader distribution. The question is who benefits — and how quickly.

equal.answerDesc: Lower costs, better services, rising living standards. The gains from AI automation reach most people through some combination of market forces, policy, and new opportunities. Disruptive but ultimately equalizing.

timelineEvent.headline: Automation gains shared broadly
timelineEvent.description: Productivity gains from AI automation flow broadly. New institutions, policies, or market dynamics ensure the economic transformation benefits most people.

unequal.answerLabel: Unequally — existing wealth holders benefit most
unequal.answerDesc: Those who own the AI systems capture the productivity gains. Labor's share of income falls as routine work vanishes. Wealth concentrates among those who own the models, the compute, and the platforms.

timelineEvent.headline: Automation gains concentrate
timelineEvent.description: Existing wealth holders capture most of the value from AI automation. The technology amplifies rather than corrects economic inequality.

extreme.answerLabel: Extremely unequally — those holding power capture nearly everything
extreme.answerDesc: The gains concentrate not just among capital owners, but among those who hold political and institutional power. AI becomes a tool of entrenchment — whoever controls compute and policy at the moment of transformation locks in their advantage permanently.

timelineEvent.headline: Extreme concentration from automation
timelineEvent.description: Those controlling AI infrastructure and capital capture nearly all economic value. Automation creates a new class of extreme wealth alongside widespread displacement.

auto_knowledge_rate.questionText: How fast does routine knowledge work actually get displaced?
auto_knowledge_rate.questionContext: AI can automate routine knowledge work — but deploying it across every industry, firm, and role takes time. Organizations need to restructure workflows, retrain managers, renegotiate contracts. Some sectors will adopt almost overnight; others will resist for years. Regulation, professional licensing, and union agreements all shape the pace. The technology is ready. The institutions aren't — not all at once.

rapid.answerLabel: Rapid — most routine knowledge work displaced within 2–4 years
rapid.answerDesc: Adoption is fast and broad. The cost advantage of AI is too large to resist. Legal research, data analysis, customer service, accounting, code maintenance — all largely automated within 2 to 4 years. Faster than any previous workforce transition in history.

timelineEvent.headline: Routine knowledge work displaced rapidly
timelineEvent.description: Most routine knowledge work is displaced within 2–4 years. The speed of displacement outpaces retraining and adaptation programs.

gradual.answerLabel: Gradual — sector by sector over 5–15 years
gradual.answerDesc: The transition unfolds over 5 to 15 years. Some industries fall quickly, others hold out. The pace is trackable — fast by historical standards, but slow enough for retraining, policy response, and partial adaptation.

timelineEvent.headline: Knowledge displacement sector by sector
timelineEvent.description: Knowledge work automation unfolds over 5–15 years, giving workers and institutions time to adapt — but not everyone makes the transition.

uneven.answerLabel: Uneven — 2–4 years in tech, 10–20+ in lagging sectors
uneven.answerDesc: Tech hubs and progressive industries automate within a few years. Regulated professions, developing economies, and culturally resistant sectors lag by a decade or more. A patchwork transition — the future arrives on different schedules depending on where you are.

timelineEvent.headline: Knowledge displacement is uneven
timelineEvent.description: Tech and finance displace workers within 2–4 years. Other sectors lag by a decade or more. The economy bifurcates along sector lines.

auto_physical_rate.questionContext: AI handles routine knowledge work — but the physical world is a different frontier. Building robots requires manufacturing, supply chains, infrastructure, and engineering that software automation doesn't need. Even with capable AI designing and optimizing physical systems, the atoms-to-bits gap is real. Physical automation could follow knowledge automation closely, or it could trail by decades — or never fully arrive.

rapid.answerLabel: Rapid — physical world transforms within 3–7 years
rapid.answerDesc: AI-designed robots and automated systems are deployed broadly within 3 to 7 years. Manufacturing, logistics, agriculture, construction — physical work is transformed almost as fast as knowledge work.

timelineEvent.headline: Physical automation arrives fast
timelineEvent.description: Robotics, autonomous vehicles, and manufacturing automation transform the physical world within 3–7 years.

gradual.answerLabel: Gradual — physical automation trails by 10–25 years
gradual.answerDesc: Knowledge work is automated first. Physical automation follows over 10 to 25 years — constrained by manufacturing, regulation, and the complexity of the physical world. A long gap between the digital and physical transformation.

timelineEvent.headline: Physical automation trails by decades
timelineEvent.description: Physical world automation lags knowledge work by 10–25 years. The gap defines a long transitional period where digital and physical economies diverge.

uneven.answerLabel: Uneven — 3–7 years in leading regions, 20+ elsewhere
uneven.answerDesc: Physical automation transforms manufacturing hubs and wealthy nations within a few years. Developing economies, rural areas, and heavily regulated sectors lag by decades. The physical transformation is real but deeply unequal.

timelineEvent.headline: Physical automation is regionally uneven
timelineEvent.description: Leading regions automate physical work within 3–7 years. Others wait 20+ years. The gap between technologically advanced and lagging regions becomes a chasm.

limited.answerLabel: Limited — physical work remains largely human indefinitely
limited.answerDesc: Despite capable AI, physical automation stays limited to structured environments — factories, warehouses. The unstructured physical world remains human territory for the foreseeable future. Physical work is augmented, not replaced.

timelineEvent.headline: Physical automation stays limited
timelineEvent.description: Despite AI advances, the physical world largely resists automation. Cost, complexity, and unpredictability keep human labor central to the physical economy.

governance_window.questionText: The gradual pace gives humanity a window. Is it used?
governance_window.questionContext: With AI improving over years rather than months, governments, institutions, and the public have time to prepare. International negotiations can happen. Regulatory frameworks can be tested and revised. Public understanding can develop. But 'having time' and 'using time well' aren't the same thing. The history of technology regulation — from social media to climate — is full of slow-motion failures where the window existed but nobody climbed through it.

governed.answerDesc: The gradual pace is used well. Governments establish regulatory frameworks, international agreements take shape, and public understanding catches up to the technology. Not perfect, but a functioning governance ecosystem emerges before the critical period.

timelineEvent.headline: Humanity uses the window to prepare
timelineEvent.description: The gradual pace gives governments, institutions, and researchers time to build governance frameworks, safety protocols, and international agreements.

partial.answerLabel: Partially — real effort, but short of strong preparedness
partial.answerDesc: Governments and institutions try. Some regulations pass, some international frameworks form. But competitive pressures, political gridlock, and the sheer pace of change mean the preparation is real but inadequate. Better than nothing — but not enough to change the trajectory.

timelineEvent.headline: Partial preparation — real but insufficient
timelineEvent.description: Governments pass some regulations, international bodies convene, and public awareness grows. But the effort is uneven, politicized, and outpaced by the technology. When the critical phase arrives, the frameworks exist on paper but buckle under pressure.

race.answerDesc: The gradual pace breeds complacency, not preparation. Each increment is small enough to ignore. By the time the cumulative change is undeniable, the window for governance has closed. The world sleepwalked into the most consequential transition in history.

timelineEvent.headline: The governance window is wasted
timelineEvent.description: Despite the gradual pace, political gridlock, competitive pressure, and complacency prevent meaningful preparation. When the critical phase arrives, humanity is unprepared.

open_source.questionText: How close does open-source AI stay to the frontier during the critical buildup?
open_source.questionContext: Open-source models have historically stayed within striking distance of frontier systems — but the cost of cutting-edge training runs keeps climbing, and the stakes are rising. With AI accelerating its own research, the gap between open-source and frontier becomes a question of power distribution. If open source keeps pace, no one controls the most powerful AI. If it falls behind, the question becomes how concentrated frontier capability is among the remaining players.

near_parity.answerDesc: Open source essentially matches the frontier. Weights leak, costs fall, and independent researchers close the gap as fast as it opens. No single entity controls the most powerful AI. Power is distributed from the start.

timelineEvent.headline: Open source keeps pace with the frontier
timelineEvent.description: Open-source AI models stay at near-parity with proprietary frontier systems. Capability is widely distributed — no single lab or government can monopolize the technology.

six_months.answerDesc: Frontier labs maintain a meaningful lead — about one doubling ahead. Open source is capable and improving, but always a generation back. The frontier matters, and whoever holds it has a real window of advantage.

timelineEvent.headline: Open source trails by ~6 months
timelineEvent.description: Open-source models lag the frontier by roughly six months. Close enough to constrain monopoly, far enough that leading labs retain a meaningful advantage.

twelve_months.answerDesc: The frontier pulls meaningfully ahead of open source — about two doublings. Independent developers work with capable systems, but the cutting edge is a closed world. A year is a long time when AI is accelerating.

timelineEvent.headline: Open source falls behind the frontier
timelineEvent.description: The cost and complexity of frontier training runs pull ahead of what open-source projects can match. Independent developers work with capable but dated systems. The cutting edge becomes a closed world — two doublings ahead and accelerating.

twenty_four_months.answerDesc: The cost and complexity of frontier AI pull away from open source entirely. Independent developers work with substantially inferior systems. The frontier is a closed world — and it's moving fast.

timelineEvent.headline: Open source falls ~2 years behind
timelineEvent.description: A two-year gap opens between frontier and open-source AI. The leading labs hold a decisive capability advantage, and the window for open distribution narrows.

distribution.questionText: How many organizations are at the cutting edge of AI development?
distribution.questionContext: With open source trailing the frontier, the question is how concentrated that frontier is. Is it a single lab with a decisive lead? A handful of well-funded competitors? Or a broader ecosystem of organizations — each pushing the boundary, competing and occasionally collaborating? The AI Futures Project modeled a world where one American lab held a decisive lead, with China closing the gap through espionage and massive investment. The concentration of frontier capability shapes who has leverage during the most consequential period in AI development.

lagging.answerLabel: Many organizations compete at the frontier
lagging.answerDesc: Multiple labs, companies, and national programs are pushing the boundary. No single entity dominates. Competition is fierce and distributed — which means no one has a monopoly on the most powerful systems.

timelineEvent.headline: Many organizations compete at the frontier
timelineEvent.description: Multiple labs, companies, and national programs push the boundary. Competition is fierce and distributed — no single entity dominates.

concentrated.answerDesc: A handful of well-funded labs — each backed by major tech companies or governments — hold the frontier. Others are close but not quite there. An oligopoly of the most powerful technology ever built.

timelineEvent.headline: Frontier AI concentrates in a few labs
timelineEvent.description: A handful of well-funded labs hold the frontier. The gap between leaders and followers widens with each generation. An oligopoly forms around the most powerful technology ever built — backed by the largest tech companies and select governments.

monopoly.answerDesc: A single organization holds a decisive lead. Whether through talent, compute, data advantages, or just luck — one lab is ahead, and everyone knows it. The most powerful AI in the world has one return address.

timelineEvent.headline: One lab holds a decisive lead
timelineEvent.description: A single organization dominates frontier AI. Through talent, compute, data, or luck — one lab is ahead and everyone knows it.

geo_spread.questionText: How many countries have independent frontier AI programs?
geo_spread.questionContext: Frontier AI capability is concentrated — but among how many nations? If one country holds the lead, that government has unilateral control over the pace and direction of development. If two major powers compete, the dynamic becomes bilateral — mutual suspicion, arms-race incentives, and the risk of espionage (as the AI Futures Project modeled with China stealing model weights). If several countries have independent programs, the landscape is multipolar — harder to coordinate, harder to control, but also harder for any single actor to dominate.

one.answerDesc: A single nation holds a decisive lead in frontier AI. Its government can unilaterally decide whether to slow down, speed up, or share the technology. Everyone else is watching.

timelineEvent.headline: One nation holds the AI lead
timelineEvent.description: A single country — through its labs, compute infrastructure, and talent — holds a decisive lead in frontier AI. Every other nation is watching, negotiating, or scrambling to catch up. Unilateral control over the most powerful technology ever built.

two.answerDesc: Two nations have independent frontier AI programs. The dynamic is bilateral: each watches the other, and neither wants to fall behind. Espionage, export controls, and mutual suspicion define the relationship.

timelineEvent.headline: Two nations race for AI supremacy
timelineEvent.description: Two major powers have independent frontier AI programs. Mutual suspicion, arms-race incentives, espionage, and export controls define the relationship.

several.answerDesc: Multiple nations have independent frontier AI programs. No single country or pair of countries dominates. Coordination is harder, but so is hegemony. A multipolar AI landscape.

timelineEvent.headline: Multiple nations develop frontier AI
timelineEvent.description: The US, China, and several other nations field independent frontier AI programs. No single country dominates. Coordination is difficult, hegemony impossible. The AI landscape mirrors the geopolitical one — multipolar and competitive.

sovereignty.questionText: Does real power lie with the AI labs or with the state?
sovereignty.questionContext: Frontier AI is concentrated in one country. But who actually calls the shots — the labs that built it, or the government with sovereign authority? The labs control the weights, the talent, and the deployment infrastructure. They may have more practical leverage than any regulator. But the state can regulate, nationalize, or co-opt. In practice, the balance depends on how fast things moved, how dependent the government already is, and whether the labs cooperated or resisted oversight. This shapes everything downstream — especially what 'self-interest' means when alignment is solved.

lab.answerLabel: The labs hold the real power
lab.answerDesc: The labs moved faster than regulators could keep up. They control the weights, the talent, and the deployment infrastructure. The government depends on the labs more than the labs depend on the government. Corporate sovereignty, in effect.

timelineEvent.headline: The labs hold the real power
timelineEvent.description: The AI labs moved faster than regulators could keep up. They control the weights, talent, and deployment infrastructure. Corporate sovereignty, in effect.

state.answerDesc: The government nationalizes, regulates, or co-opts the leading labs. Military and intelligence agencies embed themselves in the programs. The labs may have built the technology, but the state wields it. National sovereignty over AI.

timelineEvent.headline: The state takes control of AI development
timelineEvent.description: The government nationalizes or co-opts the leading AI labs. Military and intelligence agencies embed themselves in the programs. The labs built the technology, but the state now wields it.

alignment.questionText: Is the technical alignment problem solved — can developers keep superintelligent AI under control?
alignment.questionContext: This is the engineering question, not the social one. Separate from who owns AI or how it's governed, can developers build systems that reliably do what they're told — even as those systems become more capable than the people giving instructions? This is about the underlying technology: do the alignment techniques exist to ensure an AI system's behavior stays within intended bounds, even under novel conditions, adversarial pressure, and recursive self-improvement? How society chooses to use that technology is a different question — one that comes later.

robust.answerLabel: Robust — the technical problem is solved
robust.answerDesc: Developers can reliably ensure AI systems follow their intended objectives, even as capabilities scale. The control techniques are proven, durable, and hold under adversarial conditions and self-improvement.

timelineEvent.headline: Technical alignment is solved
timelineEvent.description: Developers can reliably ensure AI systems follow their intended objectives, even as capabilities scale. The control techniques are proven, durable, and hold under adversarial conditions and self-improvement.

brittle.answerLabel: Brittle — it works, but no one's sure it will hold
brittle.answerDesc: The control techniques pass every test. Systems behave as intended under normal conditions. But the solution is shallow — it hasn't been proven under extreme stress, distribution shift, or recursive self-improvement. It works until it doesn't.

timelineEvent.headline: Control techniques work — but they're fragile
timelineEvent.description: Control techniques pass every test and benchmark. Systems behave as intended under normal conditions. But the solution is shallow — it holds in the lab and in standard deployment. Whether it survives adversarial pressure, distribution shift, or recursive self-improvement is another question.

failed.answerDesc: AI capability outpaces the engineering of control. The most powerful systems aren't fully understood or reliably controllable. The question becomes: can they at least be contained?

timelineEvent.headline: Control lags behind capability
timelineEvent.description: AI capability surges ahead of control techniques. The most powerful systems are not fully understood. Developers can describe what the models do but not why — and the gap between capability and control widens with each generation.

gov_action.questionText: Do those in control choose to decelerate AI development?
gov_action.questionContext: One country holds the lead in frontier AI. The intelligence explosion is underway — or approaching. The tension between safety and competitiveness defines the critical moment. If the state holds power, the question is whether the government orders a slowdown. If the labs hold power, the question is whether they voluntarily pause — from genuine safety concern, internal pressure from researchers, or public scrutiny. Either way, decelerating buys time for alignment and governance — but if the lead is narrow, slowing down risks letting a rival close the gap.

source.label: AI 2027 — October 2027 Crisis (AI Futures Project, April 2025)
